%% Audience floor: a strong high-school senior or first-year undergraduate.
%% Story arc: high-dim data is everywhere; classical PCA breaks; random
%% matrix theory explains why; building from random variables, we reach the

%% Build status: full draft. Includes Figure 1 (low-dim vs high-dim
%% eigenvalue densities), generated by notebooks/00_intro_lowdim_vs_highdim.py.

\section{Introduction}\label{sec:introduction}

\subsection*{Why random matrices?}

Modern data sets are large and high-dimensional. A single-cell genomics experiment may measure tens of thousands of gene expression levels in a few hundred cells. A finance application may track hundreds of asset returns over a few years of daily data. A computer-vision system may compare millions of image patches in a feature space of several hundred dimensions. In each case the number of variables \(p\) is comparable to, or larger than, the number of observations \(n\). The most basic question one can ask of such data is how its variance is distributed across orthogonal directions, and which directions explain the most variation. Answering this question is the job of \vocab{principal components analysis}, and the algorithm reduces to computing the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the \vocab{sample covariance matrix}
\begin{equation}
\Sigmahat_n \;=\; \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \vect{X}_i \vect{X}_i\transpose,
\label{eq:scm-intro}
\end{equation}
where the \(\vect{X}_i \in \R^p\) are the \(n\) observed data points, assumed centered.

Classical multivariate statistics tells us what to expect when \(p\) is small and \(n\) is large. In this low-dimensional regime, \(\Sigmahat_n\) is an accurate estimate of the population covariance \(\Sigma\), its eigenvalues converge to the population eigenvalues, and, when the population eigenvalues are distinct, its eigenvectors converge to the population eigenvectors (up to sign). Methods that depend on this convergence, from PCA to factor analysis to the \vocab{spectral clustering} algorithms that we will reach at the end of this report, then stand on firm ground.

The classical picture breaks down when \(p\) and \(n\) are comparable. Each entry of \(\Sigmahat_n\) is still an unbiased estimator of the corresponding entry of \(\Sigma\) (every entry is a sample average; see Section~\ref{sec:scm}), but the \emph{eigenvalues} of \(\Sigmahat_n\) become inconsistent estimators of the eigenvalues of \(\Sigma\): they spread into a non-trivial band of random values rather than concentrating near the population eigenvalues, and the corresponding eigenvectors may carry little or no information about the population directions. Figure~\ref{fig:intro-regimes} makes the contrast concrete. The same Gaussian sampling model produces eigenvalues that concentrate at the population value \(1\) when \(p = 10\) and \(n = 1000\), and eigenvalues that spread across a wide interval, with a well-defined limiting density, when \(p = 500\) and \(n = 1000\).

\begin{figure}[h]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{figures/intro_lowdim_vs_highdim.png}
  \caption{Eigenvalues of the sample covariance matrix \(\Sigmahat_n\) when \(\Sigma = I_p\) and the data are Gaussian, for two aspect ratios. \emph{Left:} when \(p = 10\) and \(n = 1000\) (so \(p/n = 0.01\)), the ten sample eigenvalues concentrate tightly around the population value \(\lambda = 1\), confirming the classical low-dimensional intuition. \emph{Right:} when \(p = 500\) and \(n = 1000\) (so the aspect ratio is \(y = p/n = 0.5\)), the five hundred sample eigenvalues spread across the interval \([(1-\sqrt{y})^2, (1+\sqrt{y})^2] \approx [0.086, 2.914]\). The empirical density agrees with the Marchenko-Pastur prediction (solid curve), a limiting law derived in Section~\ref{sec:scm}. The phenomenon that motivates this report is already visible: in high dimensions, the sample eigenvalues are inconsistent estimators of the population eigenvalues, and the spread is a real feature of the high-dimensional geometry, not measurement noise.}
  \label{fig:intro-regimes}
\end{figure}

The mathematical framework that explains and predicts this phenomenon is \vocab{random matrix theory}, with roots in the statistics of the 1920s, developed systematically for problems in nuclear physics in the 1950s, and now a central tool in high-dimensional statistics, signal processing, and machine learning \citep{YaoZhengBai2015, Vershynin2018, CouilletLiao2022}.

\subsection*{What this report does, and how}

This report develops, from first principles, the spectral analysis of sample covariance matrices in both regimes, and uses what we learn to understand when methods like spectral clustering can recover hidden structure in high-dimensional data. The development is built bottom-up: every claim is either proved in the text, sketched with a pointer to a full proof, or cited with the cleanest reference we could find. Every theoretical claim is then illustrated with a Monte Carlo simulation in Python; the notebooks live in the \texttt{notebooks/} directory of the accompanying repository, and the figures they produce are reproduced alongside the corresponding statements.

The starting point is the random variable, the basic building block of probability theory. From there we build to moments and moment generating functions, characteristic functions, modes of convergence, the central limit theorem, random vectors with multivariate normal distributions, and the spectral theorem for symmetric matrices. Each of these objects feeds directly into the analysis of \(\Sigmahat_n\) that follows.

\subsection*{The story arc}

The mathematical journey unfolds in three movements.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \emph{The low-dimensional regime} (\(p\) fixed, \(n \to \infty\)). The law of large numbers gives entrywise convergence of \(\Sigmahat_n\) to \(\Sigma\), and continuity of the characteristic polynomial then forces the sample eigenvalues to converge to the population eigenvalues. The size and shape of the fluctuations around these limits depend on whether the population eigenvalues are simple or repeated. For Gaussian data with \(\Sigma = I_p\) the matrix \(\sqrt{n}(\Sigmahat_n - I_p)\) converges in distribution to a \vocab{Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble} matrix, whose top eigenvalue has a non-Gaussian limit in which \emph{eigenvalue repulsion} leaves a visible mark. For \(\Sigma\) with distinct eigenvalues, a first-order perturbation argument (a matrix form of the delta method) instead produces ordinary Gaussian fluctuations of each sample eigenvalue around its population counterpart.

  \item \emph{The high-dimensional regime} (\(p, n \to \infty\) with \(p/n \to y \in (0, \infty)\)). Even for the null covariance \(\Sigma = I_p\), the sample eigenvalues no longer concentrate at \(1\); they fill out the \vocab{Marchenko-Pastur law} on the interval \([(1 - \sqrt{y})^2, (1 + \sqrt{y})^2]\) \citep{MarchenkoPastur1967}, exactly as in the right panel of Figure~\ref{fig:intro-regimes} (when \(y > 1\) a fixed fraction of the eigenvalues sits exactly at zero as well, since the matrix is then rank-deficient). The largest eigenvalue converges almost surely to the upper edge \((1 + \sqrt{y})^2\), but its fluctuations around that edge are not Gaussian. They live on the scale \(n^{-2/3}\) and follow the \vocab{Tracy-Widom law} \citep{TracyWidom1996, Johnstone2001}, a limit law that recurs across many unrelated models: close relatives in the same family govern problems ranging from longest increasing subsequences \citep{BaikDeiftJohansson1999} to growth models in mathematical physics.

  \item \emph{Structure and a phase transition.} When the population covariance carries low-rank structure, a small number of distinguished eigenvalues riding above a flat baseline, a striking new phenomenon appears. If a signal eigenvalue exceeds a threshold determined by the aspect ratio \(y\), an isolated outlier eigenvalue appears in the sample spectrum and the corresponding sample eigenvector aligns nontrivially with the population spike direction. If the signal is below the threshold, the spike is absorbed into the Marchenko-Pastur bulk and its eigenvector becomes asymptotically orthogonal to the population direction. This phase transition is the bridge to spectral clustering: the same picture explains how clustering algorithms recover hidden class structure from data, but only when the underlying signal-to-noise ratio crosses an analogous threshold.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{Where this is applicable}

The techniques developed here are not abstract curiosities. The Marchenko-Pastur law sets a baseline for detecting structure in any high-dimensional covariance estimate, with applications in array-signal processing, wireless communications, and quantitative finance \citep{CouilletLiao2022}. The Tracy-Widom edge governs the distribution of the largest sample eigenvalue when no signal is present, which is the basis for principled testing of how many genuine signal directions are present in a data set \citep{Johnstone2001}. The spike phase transition determines when a low-rank signal in noise is recoverable by PCA, with direct implications for denoising and dimensionality reduction. Spectral clustering, finally, is one of the most widely used unsupervised methods for problems where the natural geometry is not Euclidean, including image segmentation, community detection in networks, and clustering of single-cell data \citep{vonLuxburg2007, NgJordanWeiss2002}. The phase transition we develop in the spiked model is the cleanest mathematical lens we have for understanding when these methods succeed and when they fail.

\subsection*{Roadmap}

Section~\ref{sec:foundations} assembles the probability and linear algebra tools just listed. Section~\ref{sec:scm} develops the sample covariance matrix in both regimes, from the Wishart distribution and eigenvalue fluctuations in low dimensions through the Marchenko-Pastur and Tracy-Widom laws in high dimensions. Section~\ref{sec:spiked} introduces the spiked covariance model, states and verifies the eigenvalue phase transition, derives the eigenvector overlap formula, recasts spectral clustering as a spiked-model problem, and tests the resulting predictions on the MNIST handwritten digits. Section~\ref{sec:conclusion} concludes with a summary and open questions, and three appendices collect auxiliary probabilistic results, simulation methodology, and selected Python code listings.

\clearpage
